ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic meatball food product class (FOODON_03302051); no Beijing-style lamb meatball specific entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Beijing-style lamb meatballs. A00LC (meat preparations, minced/ground) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for Beijing-style lamb meatballs. No suitable parent concept identified for this prepared dish.

main_ingredients: Beijing spices (star anise, cinnamon, Sichuan pepper, ginger, garlic)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Beijing-style lamb meatballs is approximately 200 g, comprising 6 to 8 hand-rolled meatballs served in a braised broth with aromatic spices.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb, ground, cooked, pan-browned — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 17290); adapted for meatball preparation with starch binder and sodium from soy sauce and dried shrimps
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base macros derived from USDA SR Legacy cooked ground lamb (NDB 17290). Carbohydrate and sodium values adjusted upward to account for starch binder (~5 g/100 g carbohydrate) and high-sodium ingredients (dried shrimps, soy sauce). Saturated fat and cholesterol retained from USDA cooked ground lamb. This is a medium-confidence adaptation; exact restaurant formulation is not available in any approved primary source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — meat and meat products category; analogous estimate from meatball/minced meat preparations
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Beijing-style lamb meatballs. GI estimated at 28 based on analogous minced meat preparations with starch binder from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. Meat-dominant dishes with small starch additions consistently fall in the low GI range (GI 20–35). Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (28 × 5.2 × 2.0) / 100 = 2.9 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg/100 g) from dried shrimps, soy sauce, and seasoning blend
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100 g per meal and avoid additional high-sodium condiments, as a 200 g serving provides approximately 1040 mg sodium — nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~28) and low carbohydrate content (~5.2 g/100 g) with high protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Beijing-style lamb meatballs have a low glycemic index and minimal carbohydrate load, making them a suitable protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or sweet sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~5.9 g/100 g) and cholesterol (~82 mg/100 g) from lamb, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate consumption of Beijing-style lamb meatballs due to the combined burden of saturated fat, dietary cholesterol, and high sodium; a serving of 100 g or less is advisable, and the dish should be balanced with fibre-rich vegetables.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beijing-Style Lamb Meatballs (京味羊肉丸, Jīng wèi yángròu wán) are a traditional Beijing-origin dish consisting of hand-rolled minced lamb meatballs slowly simmered in an aromatic broth seasoned with classic northern Chinese spices including star anise, cinnamon, Sichuan pepper, ginger, and garlic. The meatballs are characterised by a bouncy, tender texture achieved through vigorous hand-kneading of the minced lamb with a starch binder, a technique that develops the myosin protein network for elasticity. A distinctive feature of this Singapore restaurant version is the inclusion of dried shrimps (虾米), which add umami depth and a subtle seafood note to the broth. Nutritionally, the dish is protein-dense (~17.5 g/100 g) with moderate fat (~13.8 g/100 g, of which ~5.9 g is saturated), low carbohydrate (~5.2 g/100 g), and a low glycemic index (~28), making it suitable for blood glucose management. However, the sodium content is high (~520 mg/100 g) due to the dried shrimps and soy-based seasoning, which is a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with shellfish or soy allergies. It is typically served as a shared dish in a Chinese restaurant setting, often accompanied by steamed rice or noodles.
common_user_queries: Is 京味羊肉丸 high in sodium?
Are lamb meatballs suitable for diabetics?
What spices are used in Beijing lamb meatballs?
What is the calorie count of lamb meatballs?
What is the GI of lamb meatballs?
How are Chinese lamb meatballs made bouncy?
What is the difference between Beijing-style and other Chinese meatballs?

confidence_reason: No exact match for Beijing-style lamb meatballs found in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked ground lamb with adjustments for starch binder and high-sodium ingredients. GI estimated by analogy from University of Sydney GI Database data for minced meat preparations. Medium confidence is appropriate given the adaptation basis.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked ground lamb (NDB 17290) with upward adjustment of carbohydrate_g to ~5.2 g/100 g to account for starch binder, and sodium_mg to ~520 mg/100 g to account for dried shrimps and soy sauce seasoning. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked ground lamb and is not estimable from the adaptation. 3. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked ground lamb and is not estimable from the adaptation. 4. vitamin_c_mg and vitamin_a_mcg_rae are 0.0; these are confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for cooked ground lamb — lamb meat contains negligible vitamin C and vitamin A. 5. GI of 28 is an analogous estimate based on minced meat preparations with starch binder from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in any approved source. 6. Halal status is marked false because the dish contains dried shrimps and the restaurant source (field survey data) does not carry a halal certification for this item; consumers should verify independently. 7. The input description notes the dish contains dried shrimps (含有虾米), which is preserved as an allergen flag. 8. Price data (SGD 34.84) from field survey data is preserved in the input but not carried into the RESULT as it is not a nutritional field.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built complete record from scratch. Adapted nutrient values from USDA SR Legacy cooked ground lamb with documented adjustments for starch binder and high-sodium ingredients. Added GI analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database. Populated all multilingual fields for Chinese cuisine in Singapore context (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg) and confirmed-zero values (vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae) in notes. Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency. All hard gates and Q1–Q8 criteria satisfied on first iteration.
